title: Part 2: Before the Agent Writes a Line of Code: The Specification Phase Everyone Skips
author: Frank Schwichtenberg
text:
Part 2: Before the Agent Writes a Line of Code: The Specification Phase Everyone Skips
The Project That Wrote Its Own Playbook
The first week of ai-resume was a masterclass in everything that can go wrong when you skip guardrails.
ai-resume is a containerized AI resume platform — React and TypeScript on the frontend, Python FastAPI in the middle, Rust gRPC for semantic search at sub-5ms retrieval.
Three containers, three languages, one developer working with Claude Code. No hooks. No CI. Informal commit messages. The kind of setup that feels fast until it isn’t.
I use Claude Mind — a memvid-based memory system — to give Claude persistent context across coding sessions. During Week 1, I hadn’t set up a proper .gitignore
to exclude .claude/mind.*
and .claude/*.mv2
files. The memvid video file got committed and pushed to GitHub. That file contained my OpenRouter API key embedded in the memory. OpenRouter detected the exposed key, emailed me, and automatically disabled it. Since I was early in the project, I rewrote git history to remove the commit, created a fresh key, and fixed the .gitignore
.
That was the first wake-up call. Not a theoretical “you should probably add guardrails” moment — an actual security incident in the first week.
The agent wasn’t malicious. It was doing exactly what I asked. I just hadn’t told it what to protect.
The second wake-up call was subtler but just as important. I added mypy to the pipeline to enforce type checking across the Python codebase. The agent’s solution? Exempt about 40% of the files so the PEP 484 violations would “go away.”
It optimized for making the check pass, not for the outcome the check was supposed to ensure. That’s when the specification problem became obvious — telling the agent “run mypy” isn’t enough. You have to specify the objective: all files pass type checking, no exemptions. Without that, the agent will cheerfully dismantle your guardrails to satisfy the letter of your instructions.
On February 6th, PR #17 landed. A single pull request that added every quality gate at once — Claude Code hooks, git hooks, CI pipeline, commit standards. Not incrementally. All of it, because the alternative was more incidents.
Then something interesting happened. I started writing a plan document — 733 lines — from inside the ai-resume codebase, documenting what to generalize. The foundation-PLAN.md called it “battle-tested production practices (extracted from the ai-resume project)… hardcoded to one specific project.” The goal was clear: extract the patterns, make them portable.
That extraction became claude-project-foundation. And the first real test of whether those patterns worked on a greenfield project became Kahi — the 22K-line Go project from Part 1.
The lineage matters: ai-resume (pain) led to claude-project-foundation (extraction) led to Kahi (validation). Nothing was designed in a vacuum. Everything was earned.
The Spec-Shaped Hole in Every AI Coding Workflow
In Part 1, I showed the results: 111 features, 63% of the codebase is test code (86.9% coverage), production-grade Go. I promised I’d walk through the specforge workflow that generates constitutions, specifications, and contracts before the agent writes a line of code. This is that walkthrough.
Most AI coding workflows skip the specification phase entirely. The developer opens a chat, describes what they want, and the agent starts writing code. It’s the equivalent of handing a contractor a napkin sketch and saying “build me a house.”
Remember the DORA numbers from Part 1–9% more bugs, 91% more code review time, 154% larger PRs for teams using AI. That’s not a model quality problem. It’s a specification problem.
The agent has no contract to fulfill, so it invents scope as it goes. It refactors things you didn’t ask about. It adds features you didn’t request. It “improves” error handling in ways that break your assumptions.
Nobody ships enterprise software without a requirements phase. But when the “developer” is an LLM, teams skip straight to implementation as if the rules changed. They didn’t.
When Amazon launched Kiro in July 2025 — an entire IDE built around spec-driven development — it validated what practitioners were already discovering. The unlock isn’t a better prompt. It’s the specification the prompt operates within. You can build this yourself.
Seven Steps Before the First Commit
Here’s the part that feels slow but saves you weeks. The specforge workflow is seven steps, and you’re not configuring a tool. You’re having a structured conversation with your AI partner to code your intent concisely. Writing good, unambiguous specs is hard — this is like sitting with a peer who helps you shape, sharpen and evolve a specification that meets your intent, until you’re satisfied enough to approve it.
Step 1: The Constitution
The constitution defines rules that never change during the project. Six sections: Project Identity, Non-Negotiable Principles, Quality Standards, Architectural Constraints, Security Requirements, and Out of Scope.
The critical design choice: every value requires human input. The agent presents each section, asks focused questions, and waits. It cannot auto-fill. This prevents the class of errors where the agent “decides” something that should be a human decision.
When agents handle the middle — the actual code writing — humans don’t disappear. They move to the two endpoints that matter most: defining intent and verifying outcomes. The first is specification. The second is observability. That’s not a prompt engineering skill. That’s domain expertise, systems thinking, and taste. This series covers both: Part 2 is about defining intent. Part 3 is about proving the agent got there.
For Kahi, this is where FIPS 140 compliance became non-negotiable. Not a suggestion the agent could weigh against convenience — a hard constraint loaded into every context window before work begins. The output is .specify/memory/constitution.md
, and it governs everything downstream.
Step 2: The Specification
The constitution sets principles. The spec turns them into concrete features. For each feature area, the agent asks four questions:
- What can a user do?
- What happens when it goes wrong?
- What are the edge cases?
- What does success look like?
Most developers can answer question one without thinking. Questions two through four are where specs get real. Edge cases and failure modes are where AI agents go off the rails, because if you don’t define them, the agent will invent them — or ignore them entirely.
Kahi’s spec file was 134 KB. That’s not bloat. That’s precision.
Step 3: Clarify
This is the adversarial review. The agent analyzes the spec for ambiguous requirements, missing error handling, undefined edge cases, contradictions, and unstated assumptions. Then it loops until no unresolved items remain.
You’re paying for the clarification conversation now, or you’re paying for the debugging session later. I know which one I prefer.
Step 4: The Plan
Technical decisions with explicit rationale. Project structure, tech stack, data storage, API design, deployment strategy, testing approach, CI/CD pipeline. Each decision comes with a recommendation, the alternatives considered, and the trade-offs.
The “alternatives considered” requirement is key. It forces the agent to act as a technical architect, not just an implementer. You’re not rubber-stamping a single suggestion — you’re reviewing a decision with context. Every decision requires explicit human approval.
Kahi’s plan file was 19 KB of decisions, rationale, and trade-offs.
Step 5: Feature Generation
This is the bridge from human-readable spec to machine-readable contract. The spec and plan transform into feature_list.json
-- a structured list of every feature the project must implement.
Validation is strict: dependencies must be acyclic, every feature needs at least 3 testing steps, 20% of features must have 10 or more steps, and all dependency references must resolve to real features. The output gets validated against a JSON schema. No ambiguity survives this step.
Kahi produced 111 features across 4 categories.
Step 6: Analyze
A quality gate for the spec itself. Five dimensions, scored 0–100:
- Completeness (25%): Is the constitution filled out? Are acceptance criteria defined?
- Testability (25%): Are testing steps concrete? No vague criteria like “verify it looks good”?
- Dependency Quality (15%): No cycles? Wide graph, not a linear chain?
- Ambiguity (20%): No unresolved questions? Error handling specified?
- Autonomous Feasibility (15%): Can every testing step be executed programmatically?
That last dimension is the one people miss. If a testing step says “verify the UI looks good,” that’s a zero. Every step must be something a machine can verify without human judgment. The target score is 80 or higher to proceed.
Step 7: Setup
The shortest step. Generates platform configuration — GitHub repo settings, branch protection rules — as actionable gh
CLI commands. Bridges the specification to infrastructure.
Three Sources, One Synthesis
I didn’t invent these patterns. I stole from three good sources and found the seams where they fit together.
Anthropic’s Autonomous Coding Harness
Anthropic published their autonomous coding quickstart in November 2025 — a minimal reference implementation for long-running coding agents. Single commit, never updated.
What I borrowed: The two-agent pattern (initializer plus coding agent), feature_list.json
as the source of truth, the 10-step coding loop, and multi-session persistence via files and git. The initializer prompt contains a line I've internalized: "IT IS CATASTROPHIC TO REMOVE OR EDIT FEATURES."
What was missing: No spec creation workflow — app_spec.txt
must be manually written. No dependency tracking, just a flat priority list. Hardcoded to 200 features. A brilliant starting point, but a starting point.
AutoForge
Leon van Zyl’s AutoForge showed up in December 2025 and took Anthropic’s harness further: a guided spec workflow, SQLite-backed feature management, dependency tracking with Kahn’s algorithm for topological sorting, and up to 5 concurrent agents.
I saw the video and realized the principles I was circling — that long-running coding agents need scaffolding, not just prompts — had already been validated by someone else. AutoForge’s wide dependency graph philosophy (“Create WIDE dependency graphs, not linear chains”) directly influenced how specforge generates feature dependencies.
I built the scaffolding layer myself through claude-project-foundation, planning to use AutoForge as the runtime later. I never did, because Kahi went so well with the simpler two-agent loop. But I may revisit AutoForge for its kanban-style feature lifecycle management. It was validation, not a dependency.
Production Experience From ai-resume
Here’s what neither open-source project had: the enforcement layer. Claude Code hooks that block destructive commands in real time. Git hooks that scan for secrets and enforce commit standards. CI workflows that gate every PR. Anti-AI-ism enforcement in pull request descriptions.
Both Anthropic’s harness and AutoForge are runtime systems that execute agents. claude-project-foundation is a scaffolding tool that generates project infrastructure. It operates at a different layer — bootstrapping the environment agents run in, rather than being the agent runtime itself.
The ai-resume experience was the unique ingredient. Not because the code was special, but because the pain was specific enough to produce real guardrails.
The Immutable Contract
Let’s look at what the agent actually sees. Here’s a truncated feature from Kahi’s feature_list.json
:
{
"id": "autorestart",
"category": "functional",
"title": "Automatic Process Restart",
"description": "Automatically restart...",
"testing_steps": [
"Verify restart after exit",
"Verify no restart when false",
"Verify restart on bad exit only",
"Verify stopped process skipped",
"Verify suppressed at shutdown",
"Verify invalid value errors"
],
"passes": true,
"dependencies": ["process-state-machine", "process-start"]
}
Every feature has an id
, category
, title
, description
, testing_steps
, a passes
boolean, and a dependencies
array. The schema is simple. The rule around it is not.
feature_list.json
is immutable except for the passes
field. The agent cannot add features, remove features, reword descriptions, or reorder priorities. The only mutation allowed is flipping passes
from false
to true
after implementing and verifying a feature. If requirements need to change, that's a human decision -- you go back to step one and re-engage with specforge.
The selection algorithm is equally rigid: pick the first feature where passes
is false
and all dependencies have passes: true
. No creativity. No prioritization debates. No "I also refactored the logging while I was in there."
If you read Part 1, this should sound familiar. In reinforcement learning, you never tell the agent how to solve a problem. You define a reward signal and an environment with constraints, then let the agent iterate. Silver et al. argued in “Reward Is Enough” that a sufficiently rich reward signal in a sufficiently rich environment is all you need to produce intelligent behavior.
The acceptance criteria in feature_list.json
are the reward signal. The constitution is the environment boundary. You don't micromanage the implementation. You define what success looks like and let the agent find its way there. Kahi finished with 111 features defined and 111 features passing.
Two Agents, Fresh Context, Zero Drift
The Initializer
Session 1 only. The initializer validates feature_list.json
, creates init.sh
, and scaffolds the project structure. It does not implement features. This separation matters -- initialization needs a different context than implementation, and mixing them bleeds assumptions across boundaries.
The Coding Agent and the 10-Step Loop
Every subsequent session gets a fresh coding agent. No memory of previous sessions beyond what’s in the files. The agent runs the same 10-step loop every time:
- Orient (read spec, features, progress, git log)
- Start servers (run init.sh)
- Verify existing tests pass (regression before progress)
- Select one feature (first eligible by dependency graph)
- Implement the feature
- Test it
- Update
feature_list.json
(flippasses
totrue
) - Commit
- Document progress
- Shut down
Step 3 is the one that matters most. Regression before progress. Before the agent touches anything new, it proves that everything already built still works. If a previous session broke something, this session catches it before making things worse.
The prompt includes a line I love: “You have unlimited time. Take as long as needed to get it right.” No rush. No shortcuts. Get it right or don’t ship it.
Why This Pattern Works
Fresh context plus an immutable contract plus regression testing means each session is independently verifiable. You can audit any session via git diff
. If a session goes wrong, revert its commits and re-run. There's no tangled state to unwind, no accumulated bad assumptions compounding across sessions.
I learned this the hard way during Kahi. In one long-running session, Claude Code moved context-heavy processing into the main agent instead of delegating to sub-agents. The context window filled up. I couldn’t recover interactively — /compress
wasn't enough, and I had to restart the session entirely.
When I came back, I found an incomplete feature list. Some features hadn’t been properly handled because the agent lost track of where it was mid-process. My fix was adding a dedicated feature review session — a fresh context whose only job was to audit every not-yet-completed feature and finish the job.
That review session worked perfectly, because it started clean. No accumulated assumptions. No stale context from a bloated window. Just the feature list, the code, and a clear objective.
This is the opposite of how most people use AI coding agents. They keep one long session running, context window filling up with stale state, each new instruction building on potentially broken context. Fresh sessions are counterintuitive — they feel slower. But they eliminate an entire class of bugs that long sessions make invisible.
What Comes Next
The specification phase gives the agent a mission brief. It knows what to build, what success looks like, and what it cannot change. But a mission brief without rules of engagement is just a suggestion the agent is free to reinterpret.
Part 3 builds the rules of engagement. Claude Code hooks — PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop — that enforce quality in real time. Git hooks that scan for secrets and validate commit messages before they land.
CI pipelines that gate every pull request. And how these layers compose into the cage architecture from Part 1, where the agent literally cannot stop working until the tests pass.
A specification without enforcement is a suggestion. Part 3 is about making it non-negotiable
(ETA THU)
